linkedin_post.text:
A useful OSS repo is not enough by itself.

If you want free tools to drive business, the packaging has to do more than explain installation. It has to create momentum.

The funnel I would use is simple:
- short demo content that shows the config working
- GitHub as the trust layer and install surface
- newsletter issues that break down architecture and tradeoffs
- paid course for the full system
- cohort for implementation help

The key shift is demo-first instead of readme-first.

The free tool is the proof. The paid product is the explanation, context, and implementation support around it.

That is how open source starts compounding instead of sitting there as a nice repo with no commercial follow-through.

Tools: https://maxtechera.com/tools

The demo should sell the OSS repo

Most OSS tools are packaged backwards. They start with explanation when they should start with proof.

The stronger funnel is:

  1. Show the config running in short-form content
  2. Send people to GitHub to copy the base
  3. Use the newsletter to explain architecture and tradeoffs
  4. Sell the course as the full system
  5. Offer the cohort for implementation help

Explore the free tools

The free tool is not the side quest. It is the proof that earns the right to the paid offer.

blog_post.title: Demo-first OSS tools funnel for course conversions
blog_post.meta_description: How to turn open source tools into a demo-first funnel that drives GitHub installs, newsletter signups, course sales, and cohort applications.
blog_post.body_md:
# Demo-first OSS tools funnel

Most OSS tools are marketed like documentation projects. That leaves demand on the table.

The stronger model is demo-first: show the tool working, send people to GitHub, use the newsletter to deepen the relationship, then sell the full architecture through the course and implementation support through the cohort.

## The funnel

1. Short-form demo content proves the tool works
2. GitHub captures install intent
3. Newsletter captures recurring attention
4. Course sells the full system design
5. Cohort sells implementation support

## Why this works

Free tools reduce skepticism. They let the audience test the logic before they buy the explanation. That makes the course feel like the natural next layer, not a disconnected offer.

## Tool angles

- `/memory`: the pain is repeated context loss
- `/ship`: the pain is launch chaos between idea and assets
- `/orch`: the pain is delegated work without verification

## CTA

Start with the free tools at https://maxtechera.com/tools

faq.a: Because the demo creates desire faster than documentation. The readme helps after interest exists.

faq.q: What does the course sell if the tools are free?
faq.a: The course sells architecture, sequencing, adaptation, and implementation logic around the tools.
